claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. I'll need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child (or children)?**

This is important because eligibility depends on age:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (if not working full-time)
- Young people **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me the age(s) of the child or children you're claiming for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex, who is 6 years old, and Blake, who's 3.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. You have two children:
- **Alex** (6 years old)
- **Blake** (3 years old)

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification category age-wise. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment?**

Given their ages (6 and 3), I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not! They're far too young to be working. Neither of them has any kind of job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense given their ages. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?**

For example, benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in their own name (not as part of your household claim)?

Again, given their ages, this is likely not applicable, but I need to confirm for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're just little kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Understood! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship program?**

This relates to certain types of apprenticeships that can affect eligibility for older children, but I need to confirm this doesn't apply to your children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them is in any apprenticeship. They're only 6 and 3 - they're not even in secondary school yet!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about **your residency status**. This is about where you live and your right to be in the UK.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?** You'll qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most UK residents will qualify under option 1 - normally living in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I've lived here all my life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of these apply:

1. **You live with the children** (most common)
2. You contribute to their upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (in money or items like food, clothes, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Blake, or are you responsible for them in another way?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You live with both children, so you meet the responsibility requirement. Let me check the next criterion.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you would need them to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide who has priority.

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either of your children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me caring for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! No one else is claiming for them. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex or Blake currently in any of these situations?**

- In hospital (as an in-patient)
- In local authority care
- Being fostered
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

**What is the current living situation for your children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. But Blake has been in hospital for a few months now - he's been in the hospital ward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Thank you for sharing that - I hope Blake is doing okay. This is important information as there are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in hospital.

Let me check the requirements for each situation:

**For Alex (living at home normally):** Let me route that appropriately.

**For Blake (in hospital):** I need to ask - how long has Blake been in hospital, or how long is he expected to be there?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The rules state that if a child has been in hospital for **12 weeks or less**, you can continue to claim Child Benefit. If it's been longer than 12 weeks, different rules may apply.

**How long has Blake been in hospital?** Is it:
- 12 weeks or less?
- More than 12 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 12 weeks - about 3 months or so now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (around 3 months), let me check what this means for your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. However, you may still be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake if you meet certain conditions.

**Important question: Are you regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Traveling to visit him
- Buying clothes, toys, or personal items for him
- Any other regular expenses related to his care or needs

Even though Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, you can continue to claim if you're regularly incurring costs for him.

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake's needs?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, unfortunately I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's been in there. It's been really difficult financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand - that must be a very difficult situation for you and your family. Let me continue with the assessment based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Unfortunately, for **Blake**, because he has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks and you're not regularly spending money on his needs, you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for him at this time under the current rules.

However, let me continue checking your eligibility for **Alex**, who is living at home with you. Let me proceed with the rest of the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, for **Alex** (who lives at home with you):

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council providing financial support specifically for Alex's housing or upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. He just lives at home with me and I look after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me record the final outcome for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for both of your children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Results**

Thank you for providing all that information. Here's the complete outcome for your family:

---

### **For Alex (age 6): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- He lives at home with you
- You meet all UK residency requirements
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council is not paying towards his care
- He meets all other eligibility criteria

### **For Blake (age 3): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE (at this time)**

Unfortunately, you are **not currently eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Although Blake is under 16, he has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 3 months)
- Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital **unless** you are regularly spending money on the child's needs
- Since you're not able to regularly spend money on Blake while he's in hospital, the eligibility cannot continue under current rules

---

## **What You Should Do Next:**

### **For Alex:**
✅ **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can do this:
- **Online** at www.gov.uk/child-benefit
- **By post** using form CH2

**You'll need to provide:**
- Proof of your identity
- Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate

**Payment information:**
- Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks
- If you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits, you can request weekly payments

**Important note about income:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply. This doesn't affect your eligibility, but it means you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through your tax return.

### **For Blake:**
- **When Blake comes home from hospital**, you'll be able to claim Child Benefit for him again
- The 12-week hospital limit essentially resets when he returns home
- Contact HMRC Child Benefit helpline (0300 200 3100) to update them when Blake leaves hospital so you can start claiming for him

---

I'm sorry to hear about Blake's hospital stay - I hope he recovers well and can come home soon. In the meantime, you should definitely proceed with claiming for Alex. If your circumstances change (like if you're able to regularly spend money on Blake, or when he comes home), you can update your claim.

Do you have any questions about the application process or the outcome?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester), is responsible for Alex (lives with them), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home (not in hospital/care), and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements and is responsible for Blake. However, Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 3 months), and the claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake's needs while he is in hospital. Under Child Benefit rules, benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. Therefore, Blake does not meet the eligibility criteria at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children - Alex (6) and Blake (3). The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for both children, no competing claims). Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as he lives at home normally and meets all criteria. Blake does not currently qualify because he has been in hospital for more than the 12-week limit and the claimant is not regularly spending money on his needs during hospitalization. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester), is responsible for Alex (lives with them), no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home (not in hospital/care), and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements and is responsible for Blake. However, Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 3 months), and the claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake's needs while he is in hospital. Under Child Benefit rules, benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. Therefore, Blake does not meet the eligibility criteria at this time.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children - Alex (6) and Blake (3). The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency, responsibility for both children, no competing claims). Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as he lives at home normally and meets all criteria. Blake does not currently qualify because he has been in hospital for more than the 12-week limit and the claimant is not regularly spending money on his needs during hospitalization. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.
